title: Credential Compromise: Proliferation Across Distinct Billing Addresses
narrative: Stolen card credentials are being monetized by multiple fraudsters or drop-ship operations, each using different billing addresses to evade simple duplicate-address blocks. The card appears on transactions with an unusually high count of distinct billing addresses in recent history, far exceeding what a legitimate cardholder would accumulate. Individual transaction amounts and velocity appear normal, masking the underlying card compromise. The fraud is detectable through card-level aggregation features showing the card associated with billing addresses across many zip codes, cities, or street addresses that have no coherent relationship to the cardholder's established identity profile. Device fingerprints may vary as different actors use the stolen credentials from separate devices, but per-transaction behavioral signals remain benign to evade rule-based filters.
indicators: C11 > 27 (p90), indicating card has appeared with exceptionally high distinct billing-address count
card1_distinct_addr1 > 8, showing card used with numerous different addr1 values in rolling window
C13 > 24 (p90), elevated distinct card-address pairing counter exceeding normal cardholder baseline
D1 = 0 or D2 = 0, first-time device-card or device-address pairing for this transaction
addr1_card1_ratio > 4.5, indicating billing address has unusually many cards associated with it
TransactionAmt between 60 and 200, within median cardholder spend range to avoid amount-based detection
indicator_explanations: Card has been linked to far more billing addresses than typical legitimate usage patterns
The card appears with many different street addresses across recent transaction history
Aggregate card-address combinations exceed what a single cardholder would generate organically
Device has never been seen with this card or billing address before
Billing address shows association with multiple cards suggesting drop-ship or fraud-mill operation
Transaction amount fits normal spending patterns making it harder to flag on value alone
summary: Stolen card credentials are being used across multiple distinct billing addresses by different fraudsters or coordinated networks, creating an abnormally high address-diversity signature on card-level aggregations while individual transaction behavior remains unremarkable.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders rarely use their card with more than two or three billing addresses over short rolling windows. High C11, C13, and distinct-address counters reveal the card is being exploited across geographically dispersed operations, a pattern consistent with credential theft and resale to multiple fraud actors.
